Summary: This study aimed to develop and compare a radiomics model and a fusion model, incorporating serum fibrosis biomarkers (APRI and FIB-4) with radiomics features extracted from multiparametric MRI, for staging liver fibrosis in patients with chronic liver disease. The results showed that both models exhibited feasibility for staging liver fibrosis, with the fusion model demonstrating improved diagnostic performance in differentiating F1-3 vs. F4 and F1-2 vs. F4 in the testing cohort.

Item1.reason: The study does not explicitly mention adherence to any specific radiomics or machine learning checklists or guidelines.

Item2.reason: The study clearly defines inclusion and exclusion criteria for patient selection, contributing to a representative study population.

Item3.reason: Histopathologic examination of liver tissue is used as the reference standard for liver fibrosis staging, which is a well-established and robust method.

Item4.reason: The study involves data from a single institution.

Item5.reason: The study uses multiparametric MRI, a clinically translatable imaging technique, for radiomics analysis.

Item6.reason: The imaging protocol with acquisition parameters is described in detail in Table 2, ensuring replicability of the method.

Item7.reason: The interval between imaging and histopathologic examination (reference standard) was within 4 weeks, which is appropriate for validating the presence or absence of liver fibrosis at the time of imaging.

Condition1.reason: The study includes segmentation in the form of bounding boxes drawn on MR images to define regions of interest (ROIs) for feature extraction.

Condition2.reason: The segmentation was performed manually by a radiologist, not in a fully automated manner.

Item8.reason: The segmentation methodology is transparently described, including the use of bounding boxes to define ROIs in the right hepatic lobe, excluding major vessels and hepatic masses.

Item9.reason: The segmentation was performed manually, not in a fully automated manner, so this item is not applicable.

Item10.reason: The segmentation masks were produced by a single reader, which better reflects clinical practice.

Condition3.reason: The study extracts hand-crafted radiomic features using SlicerRadiomics.

Item11.reason: The study mentions the use of z-score normalization for post-processing radiomics features, which is an appropriate image preprocessing technique. However, more details on other preprocessing steps would be beneficial.

Item12.reason: The study uses SlicerRadiomics, a standardized software, for feature extraction. The version number (v.5.0.3) is also provided.

Item13.reason: The study describes the feature types (first-order statistics, GLCM, GLDM, GLRLM, GLSZM, NGTDM, shape, and shape 2D) and mentions the use of a default configuration statement. However, a file presenting the complete configuration of these settings would be ideal.

Condition4.reason: The study includes tabular data in the form of radiomics features and serum fibrosis biomarkers.

Condition5.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning pipeline.

Item14.reason: The study removes non-robust features by evaluating intra- and inter-observer agreement using ICC and selecting features with ICC > 0.8.

Item15.reason: The study removes redundant features using univariate analysis and LASSO for feature selection.

Item16.reason: The study does not provide a clear justification for the appropriateness of the dimensionality compared to the data size. It is not demonstrated by statistical means or empirical evidence.

Item17.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning pipeline, so this item is not applicable.

Item18.reason: The study mentions random division into training and testing cohorts. However, more details on stratification and handling of data processing blinded to the test set would be beneficial.

Item19.reason: The study does not explicitly address the handling of potential confounding factors.

Item20.reason: The study uses appropriate performance evaluation metrics such as AUC for ROC analysis, sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy.

Item21.reason: The study includes uncertainty measures in the form of 95% confidence intervals (CI) for AUC values.

Item22.reason: The study does not assess the calibration of the final model.

Item23.reason: The study uses multiparametric MRI without a clear justification for its superiority over uni-parametric imaging or a formal comparison of their performance.

Item24.reason: The study does not include a non-radiomic method for comparison or provide proof of added clinical value.

Item25.reason: The study does not compare the radiomics model with a simple baseline reference model.

Item26.reason: The model is tested on an internal, independent test set.

Item27.reason: The model is not tested on external data from other institutions.

Item28.reason: The study does not mention any data availability.

Item29.reason: The study does not mention any code availability.

Item30.reason: The study does not mention any model availability.
